lesson.menu_desc: Field tough questions under pressure: read a loaded question, buy a moment to think, hedge an answer you are not certain about, clarify with indirect questions, and defer cleanly when you cannot commit — with question forms, hedging language, and time-buying phrases — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 — Handling Q&A — Tough Questions Under Pressure
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 13 — Handling Q&A

listenings.text: Right, the slides are done, but the real test is the Q and A at the end. Last quarter a board member fired a loaded question at me and I answered off the cuff, before I had fully understood it, and I overcommitted. Not again. This time I have a plan for the hard ones. First, I buy a moment: that is a fair question, let me think for a second. Then, if it is fuzzy, I clarify with an indirect question, something like, could you say a little more about what you mean by exposure? Once I am sure what they are asking, I answer, but I hedge anything I am not certain about. As far as I can tell, the risk is contained, but let me confirm the exact figure and circle back. If they try to pin me down on a number I do not have, I give a ballpark and defer the precise one: roughly fifteen percent, though I would not want to commit to that figure until I have checked. And if a question is presumptuous, built on something that is not true, I push back gently before I answer it. Calm, clear, honest. A good answer under pressure beats a fast one.

Hi Victor, it is Larissa. A quick voice note before your Q and A session, because the questions are where people really judge you. Three things. First, never answer a question you have not fully understood. If it is loaded or vague, slow down and clarify: could you clarify what you mean, or are you asking about cost or about timing? A good clarifying question buys you time and earns you respect. Second, hedge honestly. You will not have every number in your head, and that is fine. Say, to the best of my knowledge, or, as far as I can tell, and offer to circle back with the exact figure. People trust a careful answer far more than a confident guess that turns out wrong. Third, you can defer, but do it cleanly. That is a fair question, let me get you the precise number after the session, is a strong answer, not a weak one. And when a question is presumptuous, when it assumes something that is not true, correct the premise gently before you answer. Stay calm, keep your qualifiers, and remember, you are allowed a few seconds of silence to think. You have got this.
